peer_exemplars.reasoning: High libertarianism and anti-censorship orientation support transparency, but low openness and high trust in institutions temper enthusiasm.

High trust in institutions and science, combined with very high agreeableness and openness, supports moderate backing for transparency, but very low conscientiousness and libertarian anti-censorship leanings temper enthusiasm for mandated disclosure.

High openness to experience and strong moral concerns about liberty/anti-domination drive moderate support for algorithmic transparency, yet high trust in institutions and a traditional-authoritarian nationalist worldview temper enthusiasm for regulatory mandates.

High openness and very high agreeableness, combined with moderate anti-censorship orientation (36.36) and strong authoritarian law-and-order preference (76.17), produce cautious support for transparency as a check on platform power without endorsing heavy-handed regulation.

High libertarian anti-censorship orientation (73.39) and skepticism of state control (71.8) temper support, but strong authoritarian law-and-order preference (81.76) and dangerous-worldview (84.1) drive moderate backing for transparency to curb perceived manipulation.

High openness and very high extraversion, combined with low agreeableness and strong anti-domination values, drive support for transparency as a check on opaque power, but moderate trust in institutions tempers it to a clear but not maximal stance.
High authoritarianism and low openness to experience lead to skepticism of algorithmic transparency as it may undermine social control.
High openness and very low dangerous-worldview drive curiosity about algorithmic effects, but strong system justification and moderate libertarian anti-censorship temper support to a mild level.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Strong dangerous worldview and high trust in government align with support for disclosure to combat manipulation, though libertarian skepticism of control moderates.

Strong authoritarian law-and-order preferences and high institutional trust drive clear support for exposing inauthentic behavior, though low libertarian civil liberties orientation and high collective narcissism slightly moderate the score.

A very high agreeableness and care-harm morality support disclosure to protect users, but deep libertarian-authoritarian anti-censorship orientation (10.5%) and skepticism of state monitoring create ambivalence, yielding mild support.

Very high agreeableness and high openness, paired with strong authoritarian support for obedience (72.2) and punitive attitudes (87.19), drive clear support for disclosure to protect public discourse from manipulation.

Very high authoritarian acceptance of coercive social control (88.41) and punitive attitudes (79.87) combine with low agreeableness (29.48) to produce clear support for exposing deceptive actors, though libertarian privacy concerns (61.69) prevent maximal endorsement.

Very high openness and high internal political efficacy, paired with a dangerous-worldview score near zero, produce moderate support for disclosure as a pragmatic tool, though low trust in others and low agreeableness limit enthusiasm.
Moderate support due to high trust in institutions and fairness concerns, but tempered by low agreeableness and libertarian anti-censorship leanings.
High fairness/reciprocity and low competitive-jungle worldview favor transparency, while moderate authoritarian submission and high system justification keep support clear but not maximal.

peer_exemplars.reasoning: Moderate support due to high trust in experts and education, but low openness and agreeableness reduce enthusiasm for broad educational programs.

Very high agreeableness, high openness, and strong GAL-TAN participatory democracy preference align with robust support for educational interventions, while very low conscientiousness and low digital confidence prevent maximal endorsement.

High openness (73.07) and very high conscientiousness (78.51) combine with strong moral fairness/equality concerns to produce clear support for educational solutions, while populist people-centrism (94.32) aligns with empowering citizens.
High openness to experience (72.97) and very high agreeableness, combined with moderate trust in science (48.76) and a GAL orientation (62.64), strongly favor educational empowerment over censorship.
